core_story.problem: The main challenge of virtual try-on is how to generate a realistic fitting effect while preserving the fidelity of the garment.
core_story.limitation: However, few works focus on virtual try-ons of orna- ments such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, de- spite significant practical demand.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction As diffusion model [9, 15, 16, 22, 24, 26, 40] becomes the de facto standard in image generation, it’s also widely adopted in the field of virtual try-on [7, 17, 32, 35, 39, 42].
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction As diffusion model [9, 15, 16, 22, 24, 26, 40] becomes the de facto standard in image generation, it’s also widely adopted in the field of virtual try-on [7, 17, 32, 35, 39, 42]. Given a reference image of an item and a target image of a model, the task is to get a preview of the fitting effect us- ing image generation methods. Since no in-person wearing or physical fitting room is required, it has great potential for massive advertising materials generation in various applica- tions of retail, e-commerce, and advertisement. The main challenge of virtual try-on is how to generate a realistic fitting effect while preserving the fidelity of the garment.

introduction_structure.main_message: Massive efforts [7, 17, 32, 35, 39] have been ded- icated to solving the problem in the field of garments.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Massive efforts [7, 17, 32, 35, 39] have been ded- icated to solving the problem in the field of garments. The methods usually employ a network module (e.g., CLIP im- age encoder [21] or ReferenceNet [11, 16]) to extract gar- ment features, which are injected into the process of dif- fusion denoising to preserve the identity and details of the garment. It has made great progress to be widely adopted for commercial use. However, few works focus on virtual try-ons of orna- ments such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, de- spite significant practical demand.

introduction_structure.main_message: Virtual try-on for or- naments presents unique challenges that existing methods struggle to address effectively.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Virtual try-on for or- naments presents unique challenges that existing methods struggle to address effectively. 1) Most ornaments often feature intricate, small-scale geometric structures, such as rings and holes, that are difficult to preserve in virtual try- ons. In contrast, garments typically have sparser and/or re- peated textures and lack complex geometric details. 2) For garment try-ons, generative visual artifacts can blend with natural cloth deformations and wrinkles, reducing their visi- bility.

introduction_structure.main_message: However, most ornaments are rigid or consist of rigid components, making any distortion or artifacts immediately noticeable.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: However, most ornaments are rigid or consist of rigid components, making any distortion or artifacts immediately noticeable. 3) Most garment virtual try-on methods require silhouette, skeleton, and semantic maps as additional inputs, while even the coarse silhouette mask of the ornament is not easy to depict due to its diverse structures and pose- depended occlusion. To tackle the problems, we propose a method to predict an accurate wearing mask to align the poses and scales be- tween ornaments and models without additional inputs such as the silhouette, skeleton, or semantic maps. Our method also preserves structures and detailed features using a mask- guided attention of ornaments and models to preserve ge- ometric structures.

introduction_structure.main_message: Specifically, to obtain a precise pose- aware wearing mask without explicit extraction of various maps of ornaments and models, we refine the mask from an input mask as coarse as a bounding box.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Enumerates takeaways to make the paper's value explicit.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Specifically, to obtain a precise pose- aware wearing mask without explicit extraction of various maps of ornaments and models, we refine the mask from an input mask as coarse as a bounding box. A refined mask is then estimated using the intermediate features in the gener- ation, which is further used as input to predict a more ac- curate one iteratively. As the mask indicates structure in- formation, especially for multiple and tiny geometric struc- tures, we formulate the attention layers to implicitly learn a mapping between the reference mask and the ground truth mask to preserve the structure patterns in ornaments. In summary, our contributions are as follows: • To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to use dif- fusion models for virtual try-ons of various ornaments including bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces.

introduction_structure.main_message: Our method can generate realistic virtual try-on results as well as high-fidelity preservation of ornament identity. • We propose an iterative scheme to estimate a pose-aware wearing mask to significantly improve the pose and scale alignments between ornaments and models.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Our method can generate realistic virtual try-on results as well as high-fidelity preservation of ornament identity. • We propose an iterative scheme to estimate a pose-aware wearing mask to significantly improve the pose and scale alignments between ornaments and models. It also facili- tates virtual try-on applications without the requirements of various additional inputs such as silhouette, semantic, and skeleton maps. • By constraining attention to learning a mapping between reference and wearing masks, our method improves geo- metric feature preservation, especially for tiny items with complicated geometric structures, such as ornaments.

method_structure.purpose: Introduces part of the proposed approach.
method_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Presents the method incrementally from overview to components.

experiment_structure.purpose: Presents experimental evidence supporting the paper.
experiment_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Uses staged evidence from setup to comparison and analysis.

limitation_expression: However, few works focus on virtual try-ons of orna- ments such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, de- spite significant practical demand.
However, most ornaments are rigid or consist of rigid components, making any distortion or artifacts immediately noticeable.
method_expression: Experimen- tal results demonstrate that our method successfully wears ornaments from reference images onto target models, han- dling substantial differences in scale and pose while pre- serving identity and achieving realistic visual effects.

To tackle the problems, we propose a method to predict an accurate wearing mask to align the poses and scales be- tween ornaments and models without additional inputs such as the silhouette, skeleton, or semantic maps.
Our method also preserves structures and detailed features using a mask- guided attention of ornaments and models to preserve ge- ometric structures.

Our method can generate realistic virtual try-on results as well as high-fidelity preservation of ornament identity. • We propose an iterative scheme to estimate a pose-aware wearing mask to significantly improve the pose and scale alignments between ornaments and models.

It also facili- tates virtual try-on applications without the requirements of various additional inputs such as silhouette, semantic, and skeleton maps. • By constraining attention to learning a mapping between reference and wearing masks, our method improves geo- metric feature preservation, especially for tiny items with complicated geometric structures, such as ornaments.
Methodology We propose a zero-shot method for ornament virtual try- on with a reference ornament image, a target model image, 361 and a coarse bounding box.
The framework of our method is illustrated in Fig.
Conclusion We propose the virtual try-on ornament task for the first time.
Currently, our method is biased toward reference images rather than ground truth images, which lack specular reflections to a certain extent.
experiment_expression: Experimen- tal results demonstrate that our method successfully wears ornaments from reference images onto target models, han- dling substantial differences in scale and pose while pre- serving identity and achieving realistic visual effects.
contribution_expression: In summary, our contributions are as follows: • To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to use dif- fusion models for virtual try-ons of various ornaments including bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces.

notable_sentences: Conclusion We propose the virtual try-on ornament task for the first time.
However, few works focus on virtual try-ons of orna- ments such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, de- spite significant practical demand.
However, most ornaments are rigid or consist of rigid components, making any distortion or artifacts immediately noticeable.

In summary, our contributions are as follows: • To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to use dif- fusion models for virtual try-ons of various ornaments including bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces.
Methodology We propose a zero-shot method for ornament virtual try- on with a reference ornament image, a target model image, 361 and a coarse bounding box.

To tackle the problems, we propose a method to predict an accurate wearing mask to align the poses and scales be- tween ornaments and models without additional inputs such as the silhouette, skeleton, or semantic maps.
1) Most ornaments often feature intricate, small-scale geometric structures, such as rings and holes, that are difficult to preserve in virtual try- ons.
2) For garment try-ons, generative visual artifacts can blend with natural cloth deformations and wrinkles, reducing their visi- bility.
A refined mask is then estimated using the intermediate features in the gener- ation, which is further used as input to predict a more ac- curate one iteratively.
Besides, inaccurate control over wearing orientations (e.g., rotated along a wrist) leading to featured components being hidden behind the wrist occasionally.
Currently, our method is biased toward reference images rather than ground truth images, which lack specular reflections to a certain extent.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
While virtual try-on for clothes and shoes with diffusion
models has gained attraction, virtual try-on for ornaments,
such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, remains
*Completed during the internship at SenseTime Research
†Corresponding authors
largely unexplored. Due to the intricate tiny patterns and
repeated geometric sub-structures in most ornaments, it is
much more difficult to guarantee identity and appearance
consistency under large pose and scale variances between
ornaments and models. This paper proposes the task of
virtual try-on for ornaments and presents a method to im-
prove the geometric and appearance preservation of orna-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
359

ment virtual try-ons. Specifically, we estimate an accu-
rate wearing mask to improve the alignments between or-
naments and models in an iterative scheme alongside the
denoising process. To preserve structure details, we further
regularize attention layers to map the reference ornament
mask to the wearing mask in an implicit way. Experimen-
tal results demonstrate that our method successfully wears
ornaments from reference images onto target models, han-
dling substantial differences in scale and pose while pre-
serving identity and achieving realistic visual effects.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
As diffusion model [9, 15, 16, 22, 24, 26, 40] becomes
the de facto standard in image generation, it’s also widely
adopted in the field of virtual try-on [7, 17, 32, 35, 39, 42].
Given a reference image of an item and a target image of a
model, the task is to get a preview of the fitting effect us-
ing image generation methods. Since no in-person wearing
or physical fitting room is required, it has great potential for
massive advertising materials generation in various applica-
tions of retail, e-commerce, and advertisement.
The main challenge of virtual try-on is how to generate
a realistic fitting effect while preserving the fidelity of the
garment. Massive efforts [7, 17, 32, 35, 39] have been ded-
icated to solving the problem in the field of garments. The
methods usually employ a network module (e.g., CLIP im-
age encoder [21] or ReferenceNet [11, 16]) to extract gar-
ment features, which are injected into the process of dif-
fusion denoising to preserve the identity and details of the
garment. It has made great progress to be widely adopted
for commercial use.
However, few works focus on virtual try-ons of orna-
ments such as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces, de-
spite significant practical demand. Virtual try-on for or-
naments presents unique challenges that existing methods
struggle to address effectively. 1) Most ornaments often
feature intricate, small-scale geometric structures, such as
rings and holes, that are difficult to preserve in virtual try-
ons. In contrast, garments typically have sparser and/or re-
peated textures and lack complex geometric details. 2) For
garment try-ons, generative visual artifacts can blend with
natural cloth deformations and wrinkles, reducing their visi-
bility. However, most ornaments are rigid or consist of rigid
components, making any distortion or artifacts immediately
noticeable. 3) Most garment virtual try-on methods require
silhouette, skeleton, and semantic maps as additional inputs,
while even the coarse silhouette mask of the ornament is
not easy to depict due to its diverse structures and pose-
depended occlusion.
To tackle the problems, we propose a method to predict
an accurate wearing mask to align the poses and scales be-
tween ornaments and models without additional inputs such
as the silhouette, skeleton, or semantic maps. Our method
also preserves structures and detailed features using a mask-
guided attention of ornaments and models to preserve ge-
ometric structures. Specifically, to obtain a precise pose-
aware wearing mask without explicit extraction of various
maps of ornaments and models, we refine the mask from an
input mask as coarse as a bounding box. A refined mask is
then estimated using the intermediate features in the gener-
ation, which is further used as input to predict a more ac-
curate one iteratively. As the mask indicates structure in-
formation, especially for multiple and tiny geometric struc-
tures, we formulate the attention layers to implicitly learn a
mapping between the reference mask and the ground truth
mask to preserve the structure patterns in ornaments. In
summary, our contributions are as follows:
• To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to use dif-
fusion models for virtual try-ons of various ornaments
including bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces. Our
method can generate realistic virtual try-on results as well
as high-fidelity preservation of ornament identity.
• We propose an iterative scheme to estimate a pose-aware
wearing mask to significantly improve the pose and scale
alignments between ornaments and models. It also facili-
tates virtual try-on applications without the requirements
of various additional inputs such as silhouette, semantic,
and skeleton maps.
• By constraining attention to learning a mapping between
reference and wearing masks, our method improves geo-
metric feature preservation, especially for tiny items with
complicated geometric structures, such as ornaments.
raw_sections.method:
3. Methodology
We propose a zero-shot method for ornament virtual try-
on with a reference ornament image, a target model image,
361

and a coarse bounding box. The bounding box coarsely in-
dicates the wearing location as ornament wearing is user-
specific (e.g., a ring has its finger symbolism). We can gen-
erate realistic and high-fidelity fitting effects without addi-
tional inputs such as pose and semantic maps. The model
comprises two vital components: 1) an iterative pose-aware
wearing mask prediction and refinement module from the
bounding box, which improves pose alignments between
the ornament and the model; 2) a mask-guided attention
module to improve identity and detail preservation. The
framework of our method is illustrated in Fig. 2a.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
We propose the virtual try-on ornament task for the first
time. To tackle the more challenging problems of intri-
cate geometric structures in ornaments, we devise two mod-
ules of mask prediction and mask-guided attention to ob-
tain accurate wearing masks and impose geometric struc-
tures, which preserve both appearance details and geomet-
ric structures to achieve identity consistency. Currently,
our method is biased toward reference images rather than
ground truth images, which lack specular reflections to a
certain extent. Inspired by the work [38], we would like to
add more fine-grained lighting control in our future work.
Besides, inaccurate control over wearing orientations (e.g.,
rotated along a wrist) leading to featured components being
hidden behind the wrist occasionally. Secondary masks and
local feature injection into the diffusion process may fix the
problem.
